title: HIV — Initial Workup
oneLiner: New HIV diagnosis needs confirmatory test + baseline immune status + co-infection screen to guide ART start and prophylaxis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **If symptomatic (fever, dyspnoea):** rule out **Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP)** — key finding: SpO₂ <92% with minimal chest signs + bilateral interstitial infiltrates on CXR. Don’t wait for CD4.
- **If altered mental status or headache:** rule out **cryptococcal meningitis** — key finding: neck stiffness + photophobia + raised ICP. Urgent CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) and opening pressure.
- **If severe sepsis:** consider bacterial pneumonia, tuberculosis, or disseminated MAC — early empiric broad‑spectrum antibiotics after cultures.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (routine screening / incidental finding)
  - Acute retroviral syndrome (2–6 weeks post-exposure): fever, sore throat, cervical lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash, myalgia, arthralgia, headache
  - Chronic presentation: weight loss, night sweats, diarrhoea, oral thrush, herpes zoster, persistent lymphadenopathy, recurrent infections
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Lymphadenopathy (generalised, non‑tender)
  - Oral: candidiasis, hairy leukoplakia, aphthous ulcers, Kaposi sarcoma (purple nodules)
  - Skin: diffuse rash (in seroconversion), seborrhoeic dermatitis, warts, molluscum contagiosum
  - Wasting, hepatosplenomegaly
  - Neurological: peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline (if advanced)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Acute retroviral syndrome vs. **EBV/CMV** (EBV with splenomegaly, positive monospot; CMV with retinitis)
  - Chronic fatigue / weight loss vs. **TB**, **malignancy** (TB: night sweats, cough, contact history)
  - Oral candidiasis vs. **steroid inhaler use**, **antibiotic‑associated thrush**
  - **Splitter:** High‑grade fever + rash + lymphadenopathy → measure HIV RNA (viral load) or p24 antigen. If >500,000 copies/mL, acute HIV; if negative, consider EBV/CMV.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Rapid HIV point‑of‑care test (if not already done) — if reactive, proceed to laboratory confirmation
  - Urine dipstick (if on tenofovir)
  - ECG if considering ARV with QTc risk (rare)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Confirmatory:** HIV‑1/2 antibody + p24 antigen (4th generation), then Western blot or HIV‑1 RNA (NAT) if indeterminate
  - **Baseline:** CD4 count + % ; HIV viral load (log scale) ; HIV genotype for resistance (must be sent before starting ART)
  - **Co‑infection screen:** HBsAg, HBcAb, anti‑HCV, RPR/TPPA (syphilis), toxoplasma IgG, CMV IgG, VZV IgG
  - **Routine:** FBC, U&E, LFT, fasting lipid profile, glucose, HbA1c (if risk factors), pregnancy test (women of childbearing age)
  - **If CD4 <100:** serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg), urine LAM (if TB suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **CXR** — mandatory at baseline (rule out TB, PCP infiltrates, Kaposi)
  - **CT head** if focal neurology or GCS <15 (with contrast if toxoplasmosis suspected)
  - **Chest CT** if CXR abnormal or suspicion of TB/MAC
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture** (if neurological symptoms + CD4 <100): CrAg, AFB, glucose, protein, cryptococcal culture, HSV/CMV PCR
  - **Stool culture / O&P** if diarrhoea + CD4 <200 (consider cryptosporidium, microsporidia, MAC)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Confirmed HIV infection: positive 4th generation immunoassay + positive Western blot or HIV‑1 RNA >5,000 copies/mL
  - AIDS definition: CD4 <200 cells/µL OR any AIDS‑defining illness (e.g. PCP, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, oesophageal candidiasis, disseminated MAC, Kaposi sarcoma)
- **Never‑miss test:**
  - **CD4 count** — determines immune status, urgency of ART, and need for prophylaxis (PCP if <200, toxoplasmosis if <100, MAC if <50)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If patient acutely unwell (fever, hypoxia, neurological): stabilise ABCs, give O₂ if SpO₂ <94%, IV fluids if hypotensive, start empiric therapy for suspected OI (e.g. IV co‑trimoxazole for PCP, IV amphotericin + flucytosine for cryptococcosis)
  - If asymptomatic: no urgent treatment needed; schedule outpatient HIV clinic within 2 weeks
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete baseline studies (CD4, viral load, genotype, co‑infections)
  - Start **ART** as soon as patient is willing and no contraindications (uncontrolled OI, active TB, cryptococcal meningitis, age <12 weeks) — in UK practice, ART often started within 2 weeks of diagnosis
  - If CD4 <200: start **co‑trimoxazole 960mg OD** (PCP prophylaxis)
  - If CD4 <100: start **azithromycin 1200mg weekly** (MAC prophylaxis) and consider toxoplasma prophylaxis (co‑trimoxazole also covers)
  - If HBsAg positive: ensure ART contains tenofovir + emtricitabine for dual HBV/HIV coverage
  - If latent TB (positive IGRA, no active disease): start INH+rifampicin prophylaxis after ART stabilisation + LFT monitoring
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong ART with viral load monitoring every 6–12 months
  - CD4 every 6–12 months (until above 200 for >1 year)
  - Regular cardiovascular risk assessment, bone density (if tenofovir disoproxil), renal function (if tenofovir)
  - Vaccinations: influenza (annually), pneumococcal (once), hepatitis B (if non‑immune), varicella (if IgG‑negative + CD4 >200)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1L/8h if nil‑by‑mouth, otherwise oral hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if immobile or acute illness
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless oral thrush (soft, bland) or diarrhoea (avoid lactose)
  - **Observations:** 4‑hourly (NEWS2); if on co‑trimoxazole, monitor K+ and creatinine weekly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line ART (UK):**  
  - **Bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide** (Biktarvy) — one tablet PO OD (note: tenofovir alafenamide is TAF, not TDF)  
  - **Dolutegravir/abacavir/lamivudine** (Triumeq) — one tablet PO OD (must be HLA‑B*5701 negative before abacavir)
- **Alternatives:**  
  - Raltegravir + emtricitabine/tenofovir disoproxil (if TDF used, check renal function)
  - Darunavir/cobicistat + emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide (Symtuza)
- **PCP prophylaxis:**  
  - **Co‑trimoxazole** 960mg (800mg sulfamethoxazole/160mg trimethoprim) PO OD (if CD4 <200) — reduce dose if CrCl <30: 480mg OD
  - Alternative if allergy: dapsone 100mg PO OD + folinic acid; or atovaquone 1500mg PO OD
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dolutegravir and bictegravir: chelate with Ca/Mg/Fe supplements — separate by 2 hours (dolutegravir) or 4 hours (bictegravir)
  - Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF): nephrotoxicity, Fanconi syndrome — contraindicated if CrCl <50, use TAF if renal impairment
  - Co‑trimoxazole: potentiate hyperkalaemia with ACEi/ARB; highest risk in elderly / renal impairment
  - Rifampicin (TB treatment): major interaction with most ART (dose adjustment of dolutegravir to 50mg BD)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - TDF: avoid if CrCl <50; TAF: no adjustment needed down to CrCl >15
  - Abacavir: avoid if Child‑Pugh B/C (hepatotoxicity risk)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF):** Prescribing without checking renal function and urine dip can precipitate acute interstitial nephritis / Fanconi syndrome. **Never start TDF if CrCl <50.**
  - **Co‑trimoxazole:** Giving 960mg BD (treatment dose) instead of 960mg OD for prophylaxis — high risk of hyperkalaemia, rash, bone marrow suppression.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS):** Worsening of pre‑existing OI after starting ART (most common with TB, cryptococcosis, CMV). Avoid by deferring ART if active OI.
  - **Drug reactions:** Hypersensitivity to abacavir (HLA‑B*5701 positive), nevirapine (rash/ hepatitis), efavirenz (neuropsychiatric)
  - **Metabolic:** Tenofovir‑induced renal tubular dysfunction, lactic acidosis (rare, more with older NRTIs)
- **Chronic:**
  - AIDS‑defining illnesses if CD4 <200 (PCP, oesophageal candidiasis, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, TB, MAC)
  - Non‑AIDS comorbidities: cardiovascular disease (chronic inflammation), osteoporosis (tenofovir), chronic kidney disease (HIV‑associated nephropathy, TDF), liver disease (co‑infection), neurocognitive impairment
  - Malignancies: Kaposi sarcoma, non‑Hodgkin lymphoma, HPV‑related cancers
sections.Prognosis: - With early diagnosis (CD4 >350) and consistent ART, life expectancy approaches that of the general population (≈70–80 years). Late diagnosis (CD4 <200) reduces life expectancy by 15–20 years; early OI mortality is 10–25% without prompt treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Send HIV genotype BEFORE starting ART** — identifies transmitted resistance; if not done, may need subsequent regimen switch.
2. **Start co‑trimoxazole prophylaxis immediately if CD4 <200** — reduces risk of PCP by >90%. Check G6PD before dapsone.
3. **Screen all new HIV patients for hepatitis B and C** — they need different ART and monitoring.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not start ART without baseline CD4 and viral load** — you will have no way to monitor response and adherence.
2. **Do not start ART in a patient with active TB or cryptococcal meningitis** without ID consult — IRIS can be fatal; treat OI first.
3. **Do not give live vaccines (MMR, varicella, yellow fever, oral typhoid) if CD4 <200** — risk of disseminated infection.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever >38°C, new breathlessness, productive cough, severe headache, photophobia, focal weakness, rash, jaundice, or inability to tolerate oral medications.
- **Follow-up:** HIV clinic appointment within 2 weeks for review of baseline results and ART start. If started on co‑trimoxazole, stop after 1 month (re‑check CD4).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ID consultant if:**
  - CD4 <50 (high risk of disseminated MAC, cryptococcosis)
  - Acute kidney injury (Cr increase >50% or urine dip protein + blood)
  - Suspected IRIS (worsening symptoms after ART start)
  - Pancytopenia (co‑trimoxazole toxicity)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 + any neurological sign (urgent CT/LP)
  - Patient develops severe drug reaction (Stevens‑Johnson syndrome, angioedema)

rawMarkdown:
## HIV — Initial Workup
**One-liner:** New HIV diagnosis needs confirmatory test + baseline immune status + co-infection screen to guide ART start and prophylaxis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **If symptomatic (fever, dyspnoea):** rule out **Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP)** — key finding: SpO₂ <92% with minimal chest signs + bilateral interstitial infiltrates on CXR. Don’t wait for CD4.
- **If altered mental status or headache:** rule out **cryptococcal meningitis** — key finding: neck stiffness + photophobia + raised ICP. Urgent CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) and opening pressure.
- **If severe sepsis:** consider bacterial pneumonia, tuberculosis, or disseminated MAC — early empiric broad‑spectrum antibiotics after cultures.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Asymptomatic (routine screening / incidental finding)
  - Acute retroviral syndrome (2–6 weeks post-exposure): fever, sore throat, cervical lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash, myalgia, arthralgia, headache
  - Chronic presentation: weight loss, night sweats, diarrhoea, oral thrush, herpes zoster, persistent lymphadenopathy, recurrent infections
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Lymphadenopathy (generalised, non‑tender)
  - Oral: candidiasis, hairy leukoplakia, aphthous ulcers, Kaposi sarcoma (purple nodules)
  - Skin: diffuse rash (in seroconversion), seborrhoeic dermatitis, warts, molluscum contagiosum
  - Wasting, hepatosplenomegaly
  - Neurological: peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline (if advanced)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Acute retroviral syndrome vs. **EBV/CMV** (EBV with splenomegaly, positive monospot; CMV with retinitis)
  - Chronic fatigue / weight loss vs. **TB**, **malignancy** (TB: night sweats, cough, contact history)
  - Oral candidiasis vs. **steroid inhaler use**, **antibiotic‑associated thrush**
  - **Splitter:** High‑grade fever + rash + lymphadenopathy → measure HIV RNA (viral load) or p24 antigen. If >500,000 copies/mL, acute HIV; if negative, consider EBV/CMV.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Rapid HIV point‑of‑care test (if not already done) — if reactive, proceed to laboratory confirmation
  - Urine dipstick (if on tenofovir)
  - ECG if considering ARV with QTc risk (rare)
- **Bloods:**
  - **Confirmatory:** HIV‑1/2 antibody + p24 antigen (4th generation), then Western blot or HIV‑1 RNA (NAT) if indeterminate
  - **Baseline:** CD4 count + % ; HIV viral load (log scale) ; HIV genotype for resistance (must be sent before starting ART)
  - **Co‑infection screen:** HBsAg, HBcAb, anti‑HCV, RPR/TPPA (syphilis), toxoplasma IgG, CMV IgG, VZV IgG
  - **Routine:** FBC, U&E, LFT, fasting lipid profile, glucose, HbA1c (if risk factors), pregnancy test (women of childbearing age)
  - **If CD4 <100:** serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg), urine LAM (if TB suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **CXR** — mandatory at baseline (rule out TB, PCP infiltrates, Kaposi)
  - **CT head** if focal neurology or GCS <15 (with contrast if toxoplasmosis suspected)
  - **Chest CT** if CXR abnormal or suspicion of TB/MAC
- **Special tests:**
  - **Lumbar puncture** (if neurological symptoms + CD4 <100): CrAg, AFB, glucose, protein, cryptococcal culture, HSV/CMV PCR
  - **Stool culture / O&P** if diarrhoea + CD4 <200 (consider cryptosporidium, microsporidia, MAC)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Confirmed HIV infection: positive 4th generation immunoassay + positive Western blot or HIV‑1 RNA >5,000 copies/mL
  - AIDS definition: CD4 <200 cells/µL OR any AIDS‑defining illness (e.g. PCP, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, oesophageal candidiasis, disseminated MAC, Kaposi sarcoma)
- **Never‑miss test:**
  - **CD4 count** — determines immune status, urgency of ART, and need for prophylaxis (PCP if <200, toxoplasmosis if <100, MAC if <50)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If patient acutely unwell (fever, hypoxia, neurological): stabilise ABCs, give O₂ if SpO₂ <94%, IV fluids if hypotensive, start empiric therapy for suspected OI (e.g. IV co‑trimoxazole for PCP, IV amphotericin + flucytosine for cryptococcosis)
  - If asymptomatic: no urgent treatment needed; schedule outpatient HIV clinic within 2 weeks
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete baseline studies (CD4, viral load, genotype, co‑infections)
  - Start **ART** as soon as patient is willing and no contraindications (uncontrolled OI, active TB, cryptococcal meningitis, age <12 weeks) — in UK practice, ART often started within 2 weeks of diagnosis
  - If CD4 <200: start **co‑trimoxazole 960mg OD** (PCP prophylaxis)
  - If CD4 <100: start **azithromycin 1200mg weekly** (MAC prophylaxis) and consider toxoplasma prophylaxis (co‑trimoxazole also covers)
  - If HBsAg positive: ensure ART contains tenofovir + emtricitabine for dual HBV/HIV coverage
  - If latent TB (positive IGRA, no active disease): start INH+rifampicin prophylaxis after ART stabilisation + LFT monitoring
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong ART with viral load monitoring every 6–12 months
  - CD4 every 6–12 months (until above 200 for >1 year)
  - Regular cardiovascular risk assessment, bone density (if tenofovir disoproxil), renal function (if tenofovir)
  - Vaccinations: influenza (annually), pneumococcal (once), hepatitis B (if non‑immune), varicella (if IgG‑negative + CD4 >200)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1L/8h if nil‑by‑mouth, otherwise oral hydration
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if immobile or acute illness
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless oral thrush (soft, bland) or diarrhoea (avoid lactose)
  - **Observations:** 4‑hourly (NEWS2); if on co‑trimoxazole, monitor K+ and creatinine weekly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line ART (UK):**  
  - **Bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide** (Biktarvy) — one tablet PO OD (note: tenofovir alafenamide is TAF, not TDF)  
  - **Dolutegravir/abacavir/lamivudine** (Triumeq) — one tablet PO OD (must be HLA‑B*5701 negative before abacavir)
- **Alternatives:**  
  - Raltegravir + emtricitabine/tenofovir disoproxil (if TDF used, check renal function)
  - Darunavir/cobicistat + emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide (Symtuza)
- **PCP prophylaxis:**  
  - **Co‑trimoxazole** 960mg (800mg sulfamethoxazole/160mg trimethoprim) PO OD (if CD4 <200) — reduce dose if CrCl <30: 480mg OD
  - Alternative if allergy: dapsone 100mg PO OD + folinic acid; or atovaquone 1500mg PO OD
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dolutegravir and bictegravir: chelate with Ca/Mg/Fe supplements — separate by 2 hours (dolutegravir) or 4 hours (bictegravir)
  - Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF): nephrotoxicity, Fanconi syndrome — contraindicated if CrCl <50, use TAF if renal impairment
  - Co‑trimoxazole: potentiate hyperkalaemia with ACEi/ARB; highest risk in elderly / renal impairment
  - Rifampicin (TB treatment): major interaction with most ART (dose adjustment of dolutegravir to 50mg BD)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - TDF: avoid if CrCl <50; TAF: no adjustment needed down to CrCl >15
  - Abacavir: avoid if Child‑Pugh B/C (hepatotoxicity risk)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF):** Prescribing without checking renal function and urine dip can precipitate acute interstitial nephritis / Fanconi syndrome. **Never start TDF if CrCl <50.**
  - **Co‑trimoxazole:** Giving 960mg BD (treatment dose) instead of 960mg OD for prophylaxis — high risk of hyperkalaemia, rash, bone marrow suppression.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS):** Worsening of pre‑existing OI after starting ART (most common with TB, cryptococcosis, CMV). Avoid by deferring ART if active OI.
  - **Drug reactions:** Hypersensitivity to abacavir (HLA‑B*5701 positive), nevirapine (rash/ hepatitis), efavirenz (neuropsychiatric)
  - **Metabolic:** Tenofovir‑induced renal tubular dysfunction, lactic acidosis (rare, more with older NRTIs)
- **Chronic:**
  - AIDS‑defining illnesses if CD4 <200 (PCP, oesophageal candidiasis, toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, TB, MAC)
  - Non‑AIDS comorbidities: cardiovascular disease (chronic inflammation), osteoporosis (tenofovir), chronic kidney disease (HIV‑associated nephropathy, TDF), liver disease (co‑infection), neurocognitive impairment
  - Malignancies: Kaposi sarcoma, non‑Hodgkin lymphoma, HPV‑related cancers

### Prognosis
- With early diagnosis (CD4 >350) and consistent ART, life expectancy approaches that of the general population (≈70–80 years). Late diagnosis (CD4 <200) reduces life expectancy by 15–20 years; early OI mortality is 10–25% without prompt treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Send HIV genotype BEFORE starting ART** — identifies transmitted resistance; if not done, may need subsequent regimen switch.
2. **Start co‑trimoxazole prophylaxis immediately if CD4 <200** — reduces risk of PCP by >90%. Check G6PD before dapsone.
3. **Screen all new HIV patients for hepatitis B and C** — they need different ART and monitoring.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not start ART without baseline CD4 and viral load** — you will have no way to monitor response and adherence.
2. **Do not start ART in a patient with active TB or cryptococcal meningitis** without ID consult — IRIS can be fatal; treat OI first.
3. **Do not give live vaccines (MMR, varicella, yellow fever, oral typhoid) if CD4 <200** — risk of disseminated infection.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever >38°C, new breathlessness, productive cough, severe headache, photophobia, focal weakness, rash, jaundice, or inability to tolerate oral medications.
- **Follow-up:** HIV clinic appointment within 2 weeks for review of baseline results and ART start. If started on co‑trimoxazole, stop after 1 month (re‑check CD4).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ID consultant if:**
  - CD4 <50 (high risk of disseminated MAC, cryptococcosis)
  - Acute kidney injury (Cr increase >50% or urine dip protein + blood)
  - Suspected IRIS (worsening symptoms after ART start)
  - Pancytopenia (co‑trimoxazole toxicity)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 + any neurological sign (urgent CT/LP)
  - Patient develops severe drug reaction (Stevens‑Johnson syndrome, angioedema)
